author: artanokhov
title: JS API E2E Electron cleanup fails on Windows - Permission denied removing locked electron.exe/dll
signature: rm cannot remove electron.exe Permission denied|Flaky Test

failed_jobs.name: JS API (26)
failed_jobs.symptom: E2E after-all cleanup hook fails: rm -rf cannot remove electron.exe/dll with Permission denied

key_errors:
exec error: Error: Command failed: rm -rf demo-electron-app-project
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/d3dcompiler_47.dll': Permission denied
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/electron.exe': Permission denied
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/libEGL.dll': Permission denied
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/libGLESv2.dll': Permission denied
root_cause: The mocha 'after all' cleanup hook in electron-app.test.js runs 'rm -rf demo-electron-app-project' via a shell command. On Windows, electron.exe and its companion DLLs remain locked by the OS after the test process exits. The rm command (running inside Git Bash/MSYS) cannot delete locked files, causing the cleanup hook to throw and mocha to report 1 failure. The two actual test cases (install dependencies, run electron package) both pass. This is a Windows-specific file-locking issue in the test teardown, not a code regression.
reproduction_steps: Run 'npm run test:e2e' on Windows inside src/bindings/js/node/ after building. Observe that the after-all hook fails to delete demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/*.exe/*.dll.
recommended_actions: Use rimraf or a PowerShell rm -Force -Recurse command in the after-all hook instead of the bare 'rm -rf' shell call to handle locked files on Windows.
Add a small delay or process.kill() for electron before deleting to ensure all handles are released.
Re-run the failed jobs — this is a transient cleanup issue that does not affect test results.
prevention_strategies: Replace the shell 'rm -rf' call in the after-all hook with a Windows-safe deletion strategy (rimraf, robocopy /MIR, or PowerShell Remove-Item -Force -Recurse). This issue has recurred 8 times; a code fix in the test teardown is overdue.
historical_context: This exact failure (signature 85df26485ff568d8) was first observed on 2026-07-09 and has recurred on multiple PRs. Prior affected PRs include #36763, #36780, #36796, #36806, #36807, #36887, #36888.
